• "4 questions. 30 seconds." — confirm quiz specs
• Shipping benefit conflict (announcement bar vs subscription section) — clarify before design begins

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Reviewed by: Email Copy QA Agent
Framework: Full 20-section QA + Copywriting Psychology
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ (5/5)

**Fix:** Reframe for the existing customer: "Want to know if your stack is complete?" or "Curious if there's something missing from your routine?" — or move the quiz to its own email targeting non-purchasers or lapsed customers.

---

**── ISSUE 9 ──**
**Category:** CTA — "Start Saving" Is Generic

**Issue:** CTA: "Start Saving"

After warm copy about compounding routines and consistency, "Start Saving" drops the reader into a transactional mindset. Sounds like a coupon, not a wellness brand. Breaks the emotional temperature the email spent two sections building.

**Why it matters:** The CTA must be the natural end of the thought you started. "Start Saving" is a different brand's thought.

**Fix:**
• "Make It Automatic" — continues the routine angle
• "Lock In My Routine" — personal, earned, on-brand
• "Never Miss A Day" — ties back to "Never run out" bullet above it

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
**FINAL SUMMARY**
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

**Biggest copy risk:**
Zero social proof in a supplement email making multiple bold benefit claims. In a high-skepticism category, "more energy, sharper thinking, better rest" with nothing behind it reads as generic marketing noise. This single gap weakens the whole email.

**Highest-priority fix:**
Add one customer proof element near the "Why People Stay" bullets. Even one real quote flips the credibility of the entire section.

**Second-highest priority:**
Subject line. "welcome to May 💛" will not earn strong open rates. Upgrade to something with mild tension before this sends.

**Send readiness:**
Not ready to send as-is. The body copy and emotional core are solid — good bones. Specific fixes needed before production.

**Requires human verification before sending:**
• "Save 20% on every order" — confirm active subscription discount
• "Every 4th order ships free" — confirm this perk is live and accurate
• "FREE SHIPPING + FREE GIFT ON ORDERS $100+" — confirm gift offer is active and threshold is correct (4/5)

Every other bullet connects a feature to an emotional outcome. This one stops at the feature. "60 seconds" answers "how long?" but never "so what?"

**Why it matters:** A bullet that stops at feature level wastes the reader's attention.

**Fix:** "→ A routine that takes 60 seconds — easy enough to stick to, even on your worst mornings"
Or: "→ 60 seconds a day — small enough that skipping it is never the plan"

---

**── ISSUE 7 ──**
**Category:** Copy Quality — Zero Proof for Bold Claims

**Issue:** "→ More energy without more caffeine / → Sharper thinking without trying harder / → Better rest without changing everything"

Powerful claims — but zero proof to back them up. No customer quotes. No star ratings. No stats. Nothing.

**Why it matters:** Supplements is a high-skepticism category. Claims without proof trigger "sure it does" from the reader. One real customer quote — "I stopped needing a second coffee by week three" — does more work than all three bullets combined.

**Fix:** Add at least one proof element near these bullets. A short customer quote, a 5-star pull quote, or a "★★★★★ verified buyer" snippet transforms this from a claim list to a believable one.

---

**── ISSUE 8 ──**
**Category:** Flow / Audience — Quiz Section Breaks the Email's Audience

**Issue:** "Not Sure Which Solution Is Right For You? Find Out Here."

The entire email before this speaks to existing customers already on a routine: "Maybe you've noticed it," "What you're building," "Keep going," "Why People Stay." Then the quiz pivots to people who haven't chosen a product yet.

**Why it matters:** After spending the whole email saying "stay consistent," asking "not sure which product is right for you?" pulls the reader backward. It signals doubt. Disorienting for an existing customer.
 (3/5)

Wondering about what? Compare to the working title: "The Month You Stop Asking If It's Working" — specific, resonant, tied to the category. "Stop wondering" is soft to the point of meaninglessness.

**Why it matters:** Two soft lines stacked (SL + PT) = weak combined pull. Neither earns the open.

**Fix:** "the month you stop asking if it's working."
Or: "you've been consistent. now you'll feel it."

---

**── ISSUE 4 ──**
**Category:** Headline — Three Lines With No Clear Structure

**Issue:** "Welcome To May. / Don't Wait Until You Need It / Start Before You Fall Behind"

Three lines stacked, no label. Are these A/B options or one stacked headline? Unlike the subject lines (clearly labeled for A/B), this block is ambiguous. Design team won't know what to do. Also: lines 2 and 3 are near-identical in meaning — two fear-of-loss lines back to back adds nothing.

**Why it matters:** Ambiguity causes production errors. Designer picks at random, or runs all three and it's a mess.

**Fix:** Pick one → recommend "Don't Wait Until You Need It." It's the strongest and connects directly to the "Don't Run Out" CTA. Drop the other two.

---

**── ISSUE 5 ──**
**Category:** Offer / Facts — Shipping Confusion

**Issue:** Announcement bar: "🎁 FREE SHIPPING + FREE GIFT ON ORDERS $100+"
Subscription section: "→ Every 4th order ships free"

Two different free shipping offers in one email with no explanation of how they relate. Subscribers will ask: which one applies to me?

**Why it matters:** Shipping confusion at the offer stage kills conversions and generates CS contacts.

**Fix:** Add a clarifier, e.g. "→ Every 4th order ships free (plus free shipping on any order over $100)" — or confirm which offer applies to which customer type. Requires human verification.

---

**── ISSUE 6 ──**
**Category:** Copy Quality — Weak Bullet (Feature Only, No Benefit)

**Issue:** "→ A routine that takes 60 seconds"
 (2/5)

**ISSUES FOUND**
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

**── ISSUE 1 ──**
**Category:** Copy Mechanics — Rogue Colon

**Issue:** ": Not Sure Which Solution Is Right For You? Find Out Here."

The quiz header has a stray colon at the front. In the doc it reads "HEADER: : Not Sure..." — the second colon bleeds into customer-facing copy so the header renders as ": Not Sure Which Solution Is Right For You?" to the reader.

**Why it matters:** Looks like a production error. Sloppy on mobile. Damages trust.

**Fix:** Remove the leading colon → "Not Sure Which Solution Is Right For You? Find Out Here."

---

**── ISSUE 2 ──**
**Category:** Subject Line — Weak Hook, No Curiosity

**Issue:** "welcome to May 💛"

All lowercase, no tension, no curiosity gap, no reason to open. It's a date greeting, not a hook. The doc's own working title — "The Month You Stop Asking If It's Working" — is dramatically stronger.

**Why it matters:** The subject line's only job is to earn the open. Date greetings get ignored.

**Fix options:**
A) "May is your month. Don't waste it."
B) "still wondering if it's working?"
C) "the month it all clicks 💛"

---

**── ISSUE 3 ──**
**Category:** Preview Text — Too Vague

**Issue:** "the month you stop wondering."
 (1/5)
